---
title: "22 · SGD: Gradient Descent with Sampled Gradients"
subtitle: "SGD as a special case of Robbins-Monro applied to g(w) = ∇f(w) = 0."
lesson-id: "mfrl.stochastic-gradient-descent"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.dvoretzky-convergence", "t6.gradient-methods"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "drafted"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start, you should be able to say *yes* to all four:

1. Can you state full-batch gradient descent
   $w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla L(w)$ in one breath? — see
   [Tier 6, Lesson 10 — Gradient Methods](../../tier-6-undergrad-advanced/10-gradient-methods/index.qmd).
2. Can you compute a gradient $\nabla_w f(w)$ for a simple quadratic? — same
   prerequisite.
3. Have you internalised Dvoretzky-shaped iterations? — see
   [MFRL — 21, Dvoretzky](../21-dvoretzky-convergence/index.qmd).
4. Can you state RM step conditions $\sum \alpha_k = \infty$,
   $\sum \alpha_k^2 < \infty$? — see [MFRL — 20, Robbins-Monro](../20-robbins-monro/index.qmd).

If any answer is *no*, follow the link before continuing.
:::

## Touch

Imagine you are walking downhill in fog. You can compute the slope under your
feet, but only by averaging *many* footsteps' worth of soil-tilt — expensive.
Or you can pick *one* random footstep and trust it as a noisy estimate of the
local slope — cheap, but jittery.

Drag the slider to switch between full-batch gradient descent (averages
*all* training points each step — smooth) and stochastic gradient descent
(uses *one* point at a time — zigzag). The loss is the convex
$L(w) = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \tfrac{1}{2}(w - x_i)^2$ for a fixed dataset.
The minimiser is $w^\* = \bar x$.

```{ojs}
viewof method = Inputs.select(["Full-batch GD", "Mini-batch (b=4)", "SGD (b=1)"], {label: "Gradient method:"})
viewof eta = Inputs.range([0.01, 0.5], {value: 0.15, step: 0.01, label: "Step η:"})
viewof seedSGD = Inputs.range([1, 60], {value: 8, step: 1, label: "Seed:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  function mulberry32(a) {
    return function() {
      a |= 0; a = a + 0x6D2B79F5 | 0;
      let t = a;
      t = Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 15, t | 1);
      t ^= t + Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 7, t | 61);
      return ((t ^ t >>> 14) >>> 0) / 4294967296;
    };
  }
  const rand = mulberry32(seedSGD * 7);
  // Make a fixed dataset of 30 points roughly centered at 3, with spread 2.
  const n = 30;
  const xs = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const u1 = Math.max(rand(), 1e-9), u2 = rand();
    const z = Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(u1)) * Math.cos(2 * Math.PI * u2);
    xs.push(3 + 2 * z);
  }
  const xbar = xs.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / n;

  const b = method.startsWith("Full") ? n : (method.startsWith("Mini") ? 4 : 1);

  let w = -2;
  const series = [{k: 0, w}];
  for (let k = 1; k <= 80; k++) {
    // Sample b points; gradient is (w - xi); average across batch.
    let g = 0;
    for (let j = 0; j < b; j++) {
      const idx = Math.floor(rand() * n);
      g += (w - xs[idx]);
    }
    g /= b;
    w = w - eta * g;
    series.push({k, w});
  }
  const Plot = await import("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@observablehq/plot@0.6/+esm");
  return Plot.plot({
    width: 540, height: 280,
    x: {label: "iteration k"},
    y: {label: "w_k", domain: [-3, 6]},
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleY([xbar], {stroke: "#999", strokeDasharray: "4 3"}),
      Plot.line(series, {x: "k", y: "w", stroke: "steelblue"}),
      Plot.text([{k: 70, w: xbar}], {x: "k", y: "w", text: ["w* = mean"], dy: -10, fontSize: 11, fill: "#666"})
    ]
  });
}
```

The grey dashed line is the true minimiser $w^\* = \bar x$. Full-batch is a
smooth slide. SGD zig-zags. Mini-batch is in between. *They all eventually
arrive — the question is at what cost.*

## Wonder

1. The full-batch gradient is *the truth*. Why would anyone deliberately
   replace it with a noisy estimate?
2. SGD's zigzag wastes some of every step. Can the zigzag ever be a
   *feature* — something that helps, not hurts?
3. Mini-batch with $b=4$ uses four times the compute of $b=1$ for one step,
   but quarters the variance per step. Which is the better deal?
4. We just spent two lessons proving that noise *can* still converge. Is SGD
   really new, or is it Robbins-Monro wearing different clothes?

## Discover

Six problems that walk from full-batch GD to SGD and prove they are the same
algorithm with different noise.

### Problem 1 — full-batch GD on a finite dataset

Define $L(w) = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \ell_i(w)$ for losses $\ell_1, \ldots,
\ell_n$. Write the gradient $\nabla L(w)$ explicitly.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\nabla L(w) = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \nabla \ell_i(w)$. The full-batch
gradient is the *average* of the per-sample gradients.
</details>

### Problem 2 — average vs random sample

Suppose I pick an index $I_k$ uniformly at random from $\{1,\ldots,n\}$. What
is $\mathbb{E}[\nabla \ell_{I_k}(w)]$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Uniform expectation = average.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mathbb{E}[\nabla \ell_{I_k}(w)] = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \nabla \ell_i(w) = \nabla L(w)$. **One random per-sample gradient is an unbiased estimator of
the full gradient.**
</details>

### Problem 3 — the SGD update

Replace $\nabla L(w_k)$ in the GD update by its random estimator. Write the
resulting iteration.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k \nabla \ell_{I_k}(w_k).$$
This is **stochastic gradient descent (SGD)**. Per step it touches one data
point, not $n$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — SGD as a Robbins-Monro instance

Let $g(w) = \nabla L(w)$. The minimum of $L$ is a root of $g$. Show that the
SGD update is RM applied to this $g$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Recall RM: $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k y_k$ with $y_k$ a noisy sample of
$g(w_k)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The noisy sample is $y_k = \nabla \ell_{I_k}(w_k) = \nabla L(w_k) + \eta_k$
where $\eta_k = \nabla \ell_{I_k}(w_k) - \nabla L(w_k)$ is mean-zero by
Problem 2. So
$w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k y_k$ is RM. **SGD = RM(∇L).**
</details>

### Problem 5 — mini-batch as variance control

If you use a mini-batch of size $b$ instead of one sample, what happens to
the noise variance per step?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Variance of an average of $b$ i.i.d. things scales as...
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The noise variance falls as $\sigma^2 / b$. So mini-batch $b=4$ reduces
per-step noise by 4× — but costs 4× compute per step. Net rate is roughly
unchanged, but mini-batch (i) parallelises beautifully on GPUs, (ii) gives
*less* zigzag, and (iii) keeps an unbiased gradient.
</details>

### Problem 6 — convergence by Dvoretzky

State the Dvoretzky-style argument: for what schedules $\alpha_k$ does SGD
converge to a stationary point of $L$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Step conditions $\sum \alpha_k = \infty$, $\sum \alpha_k^2 < \infty$, plus
some smoothness on $L$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For convex $L$ with bounded gradient variance, the schedule
$\alpha_k = c/k$ (or any RM-compatible sequence) gives $w_k \to w^\*$ a.s.
For non-convex $L$, the same schedule gives convergence of $\nabla L(w_k)
\to 0$ a.s. — i.e., to a stationary point, not necessarily a global
minimum. **The proof is "verify Dvoretzky-shape, quote the theorem."**
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
SGD is not a gradient-descent variant; it is *Robbins-Monro applied to the
gradient*. Every property of SGD — convergence, noise behaviour, rate — is
inherited from stochastic approximation theory.
:::

::: {.fun-fact}
The 1986 backprop paper by **Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams** trained
neural networks with mini-batch SGD. Before that, neural-network training
used full-batch GD and didn't scale past toy problems. The shift to SGD,
twinned with the rediscovery of backprop, is when neural networks became
*possible*. The 2012 AlexNet paper that ignited deep learning trained on
ImageNet with mini-batch SGD on two GPUs.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Stochastic gradient descent (SGD).** Given $L(w) = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i \ell_i(w)$,
sample $I_k \sim \mathrm{Uniform}\{1,\ldots,n\}$ and update
$$
w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k \, \nabla \ell_{I_k}(w_k).
$$

- **Full-batch GD:** uses $\nabla L(w_k) = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i \nabla \ell_i(w_k)$ exactly.
- **Mini-batch SGD:** averages over a batch of size $b$:
  $\hat\nabla = \tfrac{1}{b}\sum_{j=1}^b \nabla \ell_{I_{k,j}}(w_k)$.
- **SGD:** is mini-batch with $b = 1$.

All three are unbiased estimators of $\nabla L$. Convergence to a stationary
point of $L$ holds under [Robbins-Monro step conditions](#step-cond) plus
mild smoothness — a corollary of Dvoretzky.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *One sample's gradient — noisy but free.*
> *Many samples averaged — true but slow.*
> *Step shrinks like one over k — both arrive.*
> *Noise is the price; speed is the reward.*

## Apply

### Worked example — SGD on linear regression by hand

> Fit $\hat y = w x$ to the dataset $(x_i, y_i) = (1, 2), (2, 5), (3, 7)$.
> The squared-error loss is
> $L(w) = \tfrac{1}{6}\sum_{i=1}^3 (w x_i - y_i)^2$ with gradient
> $\nabla L(w) = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum_{i=1}^3 (w x_i - y_i) x_i$.
> Run SGD from $w_0 = 0$ with $\alpha_k = 1/(k+1)$ on the sample sequence
> $I_1 = 2, I_2 = 1, I_3 = 3$ (deterministic for this exercise).

A competent solver thinks like this:

1. Per-sample gradient: $\nabla \ell_i(w) = (w x_i - y_i) x_i$.
2. Step 1: $I_1 = 2$, sample is $(2, 5)$. Gradient at $w_0 = 0$ is
   $(0 \cdot 2 - 5) \cdot 2 = -10$. Update: $w_1 = 0 - \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (-10) = 5$.
3. Step 2: $I_2 = 1$, sample is $(1, 2)$. Gradient at $w_1 = 5$ is
   $(5 \cdot 1 - 2) \cdot 1 = 3$. Update: $w_2 = 5 - \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot 3 = 4$.
4. Step 3: $I_3 = 3$, sample is $(3, 7)$. Gradient at $w_2 = 4$ is
   $(4 \cdot 3 - 7) \cdot 3 = 15$. Update: $w_3 = 4 - \tfrac{1}{4} \cdot 15 = 0.25$.

**Estimate after 3 SGD steps: $\hat w = 0.25$.** Sanity check: the closed-form
least-squares solution is $w^\* = \sum x_i y_i / \sum x_i^2 = (2+10+21)/(1+4+9) = 33/14 \approx 2.36$. Three SGD steps from a bad initialisation aren't enough
to converge — but the iteration is doing exactly the right work, and a
hundred steps would close the gap.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A friend writes "SGD with $\alpha = 0.01$ converges by Dvoretzky."
Spot the bug.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A *constant* $\alpha$ violates $\sum \alpha_k^2 < \infty$. SGD with constant
$\alpha$ tracks the optimum with persistent noise of size
$\sim \alpha \sigma$. It does not converge a.s. (See Lesson 21.)
</details>

**W2.** For squared-error loss $\ell_i(w) = \tfrac12 (w - x_i)^2$, write the
SGD update.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\nabla \ell_i(w) = w - x_i$. Update: $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k (w_k - x_{I_k}) = (1 - \alpha_k) w_k + \alpha_k x_{I_k}$. **This is exactly the running-mean
estimator from Lesson 16.** Mean estimation is SGD on squared error.
</details>

**W3.** For batch size $b$, what is the noise variance reduction relative to
single-sample SGD?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Variance falls by a factor of $b$. Standard error falls by $\sqrt{b}$. So
$b=4$ halves the standard error per step.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Compute the SGD update for logistic regression. Loss
$\ell_i(w) = \log(1 + e^{-y_i w^\top x_i})$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$.
What is the per-sample gradient?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\frac{d}{dw}\log(1+e^{-yw^\top x}) = \frac{-y x e^{-yw^\top x}}{1 + e^{-yw^\top x}}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\nabla \ell_i(w) = -y_i x_i \, \sigma(-y_i w^\top x_i)$ where
$\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$ is the logistic function. SGD update:
$w_{k+1} = w_k + \alpha_k y_{I_k} x_{I_k} \sigma(-y_{I_k} w_k^\top x_{I_k})$.
This is the engine of every logistic-regression library.
</details>

**P2.** Show that mini-batch SGD with batch size $b$ is *also* a Dvoretzky
iteration. Identify $\alpha_k$, $\beta_k$, $T_k$, $w^\*$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The recursion is $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k \hat\nabla_k$ with
$\hat\nabla_k = \frac{1}{b}\sum_{j=1}^b \nabla \ell_{I_{k,j}}(w_k)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Reshape: $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k \hat\nabla_k$. To put it in the
master form, treat $T_k = w_k - \hat\nabla_k$, $\alpha_k = \beta_k = \alpha_k$:
$w_{k+1} = (1-\alpha_k) w_k + \alpha_k T_k$. The fixed point of the iteration
is $w^\*$ where $\nabla L(w^\*) = 0$. Conditional on $\mathcal{F}_k$,
$\mathbb{E}[\hat\nabla_k] = \nabla L(w_k)$, so $\mathbb{E}[T_k] = w_k - \nabla L(w_k)$ — a *contracting* target as long as we have local strong convexity.
Dvoretzky applies.
</details>

**P3.** *The variance question.* SGD's per-step variance is
$\sigma^2 / b$. Compare two strategies for fixed total compute $T$ samples:
(a) one full-batch step with $b = T$; (b) $T$ SGD steps with $b = 1$. Which
makes more progress on a *strongly convex* objective?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Full-batch contracts per step by a constant; SGD contracts on average per
step by a constant.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
On strongly convex $L$ with condition number $\kappa$: full-batch
$T = b$ gives one contraction by $1 - 1/\kappa$. SGD takes $T$ steps,
each contracting on average by $1 - O(1/\kappa)$, accumulating roughly
$(1 - 1/\kappa)^T \approx \exp(-T/\kappa)$ — *much* better. **SGD wins
when the dataset is large because it can take many noisy steps in the
time it takes full-batch to take one clean one.**
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *Why does SGD escape saddle points that GD gets stuck in?* Sketch.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
At a saddle point, the gradient is zero. GD stops. What does SGD do?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At a saddle, $\nabla L = 0$ so GD makes no progress. SGD's per-sample
gradient is *non-zero* — its expectation is zero, but its realisation isn't.
The noise gives SGD a random push, and any push along a negative-curvature
direction sets the iterate sliding off the saddle. The 2015 paper *Escaping
from Saddle Points — Online Stochastic Gradient for Tensor Decomposition*
(Ge et al.) made this precise.
</details>

**S2.** Reconcile: Dvoretzky says SGD with constant $\alpha$ does not
converge, yet every modern deep-learning paper uses constant or
piecewise-constant learning rates. How can both be true?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
What does "converge" mean here? Two senses are at play.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
"Convergence almost surely" needs vanishing $\alpha$. Constant-$\alpha$ SGD
*does* converge — but in the weaker sense of a stationary distribution: the
iterate orbits the optimum in a noise cloud of size $O(\sqrt{\alpha} \cdot \sigma)$. For deep networks, this orbiting region happens to be of similar
size to the basin of generalisation; you do not actually want to "land" at
$w^\*$ exactly because $w^\*$ on the training set is not $w^\*$ on the test
set. So practitioners stay in the noise cloud deliberately. Theory and
practice are consistent once you read "convergence" carefully.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"SGD is *biased*."** The expected gradient over a uniformly sampled
   index *is* the full gradient. SGD is unbiased; it just has variance.
2. **"Constant $\alpha$ doesn't work."** It works in practice for tracking
   and for non-stationary objectives; it just does not satisfy the formal
   a.s.-convergence theorem. Pick a step rule that matches your goal.
3. **"SGD = GD with bigger steps."** No. SGD is GD with a *random* gradient.
   The noise is structurally different from a step-size change; it's the
   noise that lets SGD escape saddle points.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else:

1. **SGD is RM applied to** $g(w) = \nabla L(w) = 0$. Every property follows.
2. **Mini-batch is a variance knob:** higher $b$ = lower noise per step =
   higher cost per step.
3. **Constant** $\alpha$ **tracks; vanishing** $\alpha$ **converges.** Pick
   based on whether you have a stationary or moving target.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you derive the SGD update for any loss $L = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum \ell_i$
   without notes?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 8 practice problems without peeking past
   *Hint 1*?
3. Can you state, in one breath, why SGD is *the same algorithm* as
   Robbins-Monro?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Lesson 23 — TD(0)](../23-td-state-values/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Working P3 — the strongly-convex argument is the seed for
  every learning-rate analysis.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* Problems 4 and 6 — the SGD = RM
  identification is the load-bearing fact of this lesson.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[MFRL — 21, Dvoretzky](../21-dvoretzky-convergence/index.qmd)** — used
  in Problem 6 and the convergence argument.
- **[Tier 6, Lesson 10 — Gradient Methods](../../tier-6-undergrad-advanced/10-gradient-methods/index.qmd)** — full-batch GD, the loss landscape picture,
  and learning-rate intuitions.
- **[MFRL — 20, Robbins-Monro](../20-robbins-monro/index.qmd)** — the parent
  algorithm SGD specialises.

### What this unlocks

- **[MFRL — 23, TD(0)](../23-td-state-values/index.qmd)** — a TD update can
  also be written as a (semi-)gradient SGD step on a Bellman-error
  surrogate.
- **[MFRL — 29, TD with FA](../29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd)** —
  function-approximation TD is SGD on a parameter vector $w$.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** SGD trains every neural network in production. The momentum
optimisers (Adam, RMSProp, AdamW) are SGD with adaptive per-parameter step
sizes — still RM-shaped at heart. The 2017 paper *Don't Decay the Learning
Rate, Increase the Batch Size* showed that increasing $b$ over training is
mathematically equivalent to decreasing $\alpha$ — exactly because both
sit in the same Dvoretzky inequality.
See [gradient descent](/glossary.qmd#gradient-descent) and
[learning rate](/glossary.qmd#learning-rate).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this track
expands into 38 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Source:** Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement
  Learning* (Springer 2025), Chapter 6 §6.3. Cited freely; never transcribed.
- **Touchstones honoured:** Russian school problem-first sequencing,
  Better Explained "RM in disguise" framing, Bhāskara verse for the
  full-batch / SGD trade.
- **Self-study guarantees:** pre-flight ✓, multiple representations
  (widget, recursion, English, dataset) ✓, graduated hints ✓, worked
  solutions ✓, common-confusions ✓, self-assessment ✓.
:::
